course_info.title: Machine Learning Applications
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Supervised Learning: Linear Regression and Gradient Descent
learning_objectives: Derive the ordinary least squares solution for linear regression
Implement gradient descent optimization from scratch
Understand the relationship between loss functions and model fitting
readings: Bishop, Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning, Ch. 3
Andrew Ng, Machine Learning Yearning, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Machine Learning Applications builds on the AI fundamentals course to give you hands-on experience implementing, training, and evaluating ML models for real-world problems. We start with the simplest model that illustrates all the key concepts.
key_concepts: Linear regression: y = wX + b, minimizing mean squared error
Loss functions: MSE, MAE, and their gradients
Gradient descent: iteratively updating weights in the direction of steepest descent
Learning rate: too large diverges, too small is slow
Feature scaling: normalization and standardization for stable training
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement linear regression with gradient descent from scratch in NumPy on a housing price dataset, visualizing the loss curve and decision boundary at each iteration
setup_requirements: Python 3.12+ with NumPy, pandas, matplotlib, scikit-learn

review.value: The gradient points in the direction of steepest increase; subtracting it moves downhill. The learning rate controls step size. Convergence occurs when the gradient is approximately zero
Features on different scales cause elongated contours in the loss landscape, making gradient descent oscillate. Scaling all features to similar ranges creates circular contours for faster convergence

class_schedule.topic: Classification: Logistic Regression and Neural Networks
learning_objectives: Implement logistic regression for binary classification
Build a simple feedforward neural network from scratch
Understand backpropagation as an application of the chain rule
readings: Goodfellow, Deep Learning, Ch. 6 - Deep Feedforward Networks

lecture_notes.introduction: Logistic regression extends linear regression to classification by passing the output through a sigmoid function. Neural networks generalize this by stacking multiple layers of nonlinear transformations, enabling learning of complex decision boundaries.
key_concepts: Logistic regression: sigmoid activation, binary cross-entropy loss
Decision boundaries: linear (logistic regression) vs nonlinear (neural networks)
Neural network architecture: layers, weights, biases, activation functions
Backpropagation: computing gradients through the chain rule
Activation functions: ReLU, sigmoid, tanh and their properties
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a 2-layer neural network from scratch in NumPy that learns XOR (which logistic regression cannot solve), visualizing the hidden layer representations

review.value: A neural network with a single hidden layer of sufficient width can approximate any continuous function to arbitrary precision; depth (more layers) achieves the same with exponentially fewer neurons
Sigmoid and tanh squash gradients to near-zero for large inputs, causing early layers to learn very slowly; ReLU avoids this by having a constant gradient for positive inputs

learning_objectives: Evaluate classifiers using precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC curves
Apply regularization techniques to prevent overfitting
Implement cross-validation for reliable model comparison
readings: Hastie, Elements of Statistical Learning, Ch. 7

lecture_notes.introduction: A model is only as good as its evaluation. Accuracy alone is misleading for imbalanced datasets. This session covers the metrics and techniques that let you honestly assess and improve model performance.
key_concepts: Confusion matrix: TP, TN, FP, FN
Precision (positive predictive value), recall (sensitivity), F1-score
ROC curve and AUC: threshold-independent evaluation
L1 (Lasso) and L2 (Ridge) regularization: penalizing model complexity
k-fold cross-validation: reducing evaluation variance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Train a classifier on an imbalanced credit fraud dataset, show that accuracy is 99% but recall is 10%, then apply class weighting, threshold tuning, and regularization to improve the useful metrics

review.value: Lowering the classification threshold increases recall (catches more positives) but decreases precision (more false positives). The right trade-off depends on the cost of each error type
Adds lambda * sum(w^2) to the loss, penalizing large weights; this discourages the model from relying heavily on any single feature, reducing overfitting by smoothing the decision boundary

learning_objectives: Implement decision trees and understand information gain splitting
Build random forests and gradient boosting ensembles
Compare ensemble methods with single models on real datasets
readings: Hastie, Ch. 9 - Additive Models and Trees
Friedman, 'Greedy Function Approximation: A Gradient Boosting Machine' (2001)

lecture_notes.introduction: Ensemble methods combine multiple weak models into a strong one. Random forests and gradient boosting are the most consistently effective ML algorithms for structured data, winning the majority of Kaggle competitions that do not involve images or text.
key_concepts: Decision tree splitting criteria: information gain, Gini impurity
Bagging: training on random subsets with replacement (reduces variance)
Random forests: bagging + random feature subsets at each split
Gradient boosting: sequentially fitting trees to residual errors
XGBoost and LightGBM: optimized gradient boosting implementations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare a single decision tree, random forest, and XGBoost on a real tabular dataset, showing how ensemble size and tree depth affect accuracy and overfitting

review.value: Bagging trains models independently in parallel, reducing variance (random forests). Boosting trains models sequentially, each correcting the previous one's errors, reducing bias (gradient boosting). Boosting often achieves higher accuracy but is more prone to overfitting
Tree-based models naturally provide feature importance scores based on how much each feature reduces impurity across all splits; this makes them interpretable and useful for feature selection

class_schedule.topic: Unsupervised Learning: Clustering and Dimensionality Reduction
learning_objectives: Implement k-means clustering and evaluate with silhouette scores
Apply PCA for dimensionality reduction and visualization
Understand when to use unsupervised vs supervised approaches
readings: Bishop, Ch. 9 - Mixture Models and EM

lecture_notes.introduction: Unsupervised learning finds structure in data without labels. Clustering groups similar items together; dimensionality reduction compresses high-dimensional data while preserving structure. Both are essential for exploratory data analysis and preprocessing.
key_concepts: k-means: iterative centroid assignment and update, O(nkd) per iteration
Choosing k: elbow method, silhouette score, domain knowledge
DBSCAN: density-based clustering, handles arbitrary shapes
PCA: projecting data onto directions of maximum variance
t-SNE: nonlinear dimensionality reduction for visualization
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply k-means and DBSCAN to customer segmentation data, use PCA to reduce 20 features to 2 for visualization, and compare the cluster structures found by each method

review.value: k-means assumes spherical clusters of similar size, requires specifying k in advance, and is sensitive to initialization; running with multiple random initializations (k-means++) and comparing inertia mitigates the initialization issue
Each principal component is a linear combination of original features; the first PC captures the most variance. Examining the component loadings reveals which original features drive each component

class_schedule.topic: Deep Learning: CNNs and Transfer Learning
learning_objectives: Understand convolutional neural network architecture for image classification
Apply transfer learning using pre-trained models
readings: Goodfellow, Deep Learning, Ch. 9 - Convolutional Networks

lecture_notes.introduction: Convolutional neural networks revolutionized computer vision. Transfer learning lets you leverage models trained on millions of images and adapt them to your specific task with minimal data, making deep learning practical for real-world applications.
key_concepts: Convolution operation: local receptive fields, weight sharing, feature maps
CNN architecture: convolutional layers, pooling, fully connected head
Pre-trained models: ResNet, VGG, EfficientNet trained on ImageNet
Transfer learning: freeze base layers, train new classification head
Fine-tuning: unfreeze some layers and train with small learning rate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Use a pre-trained ResNet50 to classify a custom dataset of 500 images across 10 classes, comparing training from scratch (poor) vs transfer learning (excellent) with the same data

review.value: Early CNN layers learn universal features (edges, textures, shapes) that transfer across tasks; only the final classification layers need task-specific training. This dramatically reduces the data and compute required

If your dataset is small and similar to ImageNet, freeze all base layers. If your dataset is large or very different from ImageNet, unfreeze later layers and fine-tune with a small learning rate to adapt features

questions.question: Derive the gradient of the binary cross-entropy loss L = -[y*log(p) + (1-y)*log(1-p)] with respect to the predicted probability p. Explain why this loss is preferred over MSE for classification.
questions.answer:
dL/dp = -y/p + (1-y)/(1-p) = (p-y) / (p*(1-p)).

Binary cross-entropy is preferred over MSE for classification because: (1) its gradient is proportional to the error (p-y), giving strong gradients when the prediction is confidently wrong (p near 0 when y=1), while MSE with sigmoid has small gradients at the extremes due to sigmoid saturation. (2) Cross-entropy directly measures the information-theoretic divergence between predicted and true distributions. (3) The loss landscape for cross-entropy + sigmoid is convex in the logit space, enabling reliable optimization.

rubric.key: Explains gradient advantage over MSE with sigmoid

questions.question: A neural network has 2 input neurons, a hidden layer of 4 neurons with ReLU activation, and 1 output neuron with sigmoid activation. How many learnable parameters does this network have?
questions.answer: Layer 1 (input to hidden): 2 inputs * 4 neurons = 8 weights + 4 biases = 12 parameters. Layer 2 (hidden to output): 4 inputs * 1 neuron = 4 weights + 1 bias = 5 parameters. Total: 12 + 5 = 17 learnable parameters.

rubric.key: Correct weight count for each layer

quizzes.topic: Evaluation, Ensemble Methods, and Unsupervised Learning

questions.question: A spam classifier has the following confusion matrix: TP=180, FP=20, FN=30, TN=770. Calculate precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy. Which metric is most important for this application and why?
questions.answer:
Precision = TP/(TP+FP) = 180/200 = 0.90. Recall = TP/(TP+FN) = 180/210 = 0.857. F1 = 2*P*R/(P+R) = 2*0.90*0.857/(0.90+0.857) = 0.878. Accuracy = (TP+TN)/(TP+FP+FN+TN) = 950/1000 = 0.95.

For spam classification, precision is most important because false positives (legitimate emails marked as spam) are more costly than false negatives (spam reaching the inbox). A missed spam is annoying; a lost important email could be catastrophic.

rubric.key: Explains why FP is costlier than FN for spam

questions.question: Explain why random forests are less prone to overfitting than a single deep decision tree, even though they use many trees.
questions.answer: A single deep decision tree overfits by memorizing training data noise. Random forests mitigate overfitting through two mechanisms: (1) Bagging: each tree is trained on a different bootstrap sample, so individual tree errors are uncorrelated and average out across the ensemble. (2) Random feature selection: at each split, only a random subset of features is considered, further decorrelating the trees. The averaging of many uncorrelated, individually overfit trees reduces variance without increasing bias, following the law of large numbers.

rubric.key: Explains bagging reduces variance through averaging
Explains random feature selection decorrelates trees

major_assignments.title: End-to-End ML Pipeline for Real-World Data
major_assignments.description: Build a complete machine learning pipeline for a real-world prediction task: data exploration, feature engineering, model selection, hyperparameter tuning, evaluation, and deployment-ready model export. Compare at least three different model families.
objectives: Perform exploratory data analysis and feature engineering on a messy real-world dataset
Train and compare at least three model families (linear, tree-based, neural)
Tune hyperparameters using cross-validation and grid/random search
Evaluate models using appropriate metrics and produce a deployment-ready model
deliverables: Jupyter Notebook with complete pipeline and analysis
Trained model files exported for deployment
Analysis report (3-4 pages) comparing models and justifying the final choice
Presentation slides summarizing approach and results

criteria: Thorough EDA with meaningful visualizations
Missing data and outliers handled with justification
Feature engineering creates informative new features
Data leakage is identified and prevented
At least three model families trained and compared
Hyperparameter tuning uses cross-validation properly
Model comparison uses appropriate metrics for the task
Final model selection is justified with evidence
Proper train/validation/test split with no data leakage
Multiple evaluation metrics reported and interpreted
Error analysis identifies model failure modes
Confidence intervals or variance reported for key metrics
Analysis report is clear and well-structured
Visualizations effectively communicate results
Limitations and future improvements are discussed
Notebook is reproducible and well-documented
Complete ML pipeline from raw data to deployment-ready model
Rigorous model comparison with cross-validated hyperparameter tuning
Thorough evaluation with error analysis and honest limitations
Professional documentation and presentation

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of ML concepts and math

lab_assignments.description: Weekly implementation labs for each algorithm

midterm_examination.description: Written exam covering supervised learning and evaluation

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all ML topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for lectures and mandatory for lab sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of ML concepts is encouraged. All code, analysis, and reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying code from Kaggle kernels, online tutorials, or other students without attribution is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Python 3.12+ with NumPy, pandas, scikit-learn, PyTorch, matplotlib, and Jupyter. GPU access via Google Colab is sufficient.
